perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of any jurisdiction chosen by Intel Corporation.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies California law governs the agreement. Modifying it to allow Intel to choose any jurisdiction introduces a potential legal contradiction, as it could allow Intel to select a jurisdiction with laws less favorable to the Participant, thereby diminishing rights afforded under California law. This creates legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law addresses choice of law clauses in contracts. By allowing Intel to choose *any* jurisdiction, it potentially violates this section if the chosen jurisdiction has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or if the application of the chosen law would be contrary to the fundamental policy of California, thus diminishing rights of the parties in California. California law requires contracts to have reasonable relations to the jurisdiction specified in the contract. Removing this stipulation could violate California law.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.42. (a) The following provisions of a contract between the contractor and a subcontractor with principal offices in this state, for the construction of a public or private work of improvement in this state, shall be void and unenforceable: (1) A provision which purports to require any dispute between the parties to be litigated, arbitrated, or otherwise determined outside this state. (2) A provision which purports to preclude a party from commencing such a proceeding or obtaining a judgment or other resolution in this state or the courts of this state. (b) For purposes of this section, “construction” means any work or services performed on, or materials provided for, a work of improvement, as defined in Section 8050 of the Civil Code, and for which a lien may be claimed pursuant to Section 8400 of the Civil Code (whether or not a lien is in fact cl
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows Intel to choose any jurisdiction, which directly contradicts the principle that the chosen jurisdiction should have a substantial relationship to the parties or transaction and not violate fundamental California policy, as explained by the Law Explanation. The original text limited the governing law to California, ensuring certain protections. The modification introduces a significant legal risk, diminishing rights afforded under California law, which is not allowed. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address the 'choice of law' issue in general contracts, it does indicate that California law prevents enforcing clauses that require disputes to be resolved outside of California in specific construction contracts. By analogy, this reveals a protective stance under California law that is undermined by allowing the choice of any jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall maintain the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that the receiving party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall maintain the Confidential Information with a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "at least the same degree of care that the receiving party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information" lowers the standard of care. While "reasonable degree of care" is maintained, the original text established a higher standard, linked to the receiving party's own practices, which creates a stricter protection for the disclosing party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By deleting the clause requiring the receiving party to treat confidential information with at least the same care as its own proprietary information, the modified text might be construed as failing to meet the 'reasonable measures' standard, particularly if the receiving party has lax security practices for its own confidential information. Thus, the omission creates a contradiction with the spirit and intent of CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a standard of care tied to the receiving party's own practices, but no less than a reasonable degree of care. The changed text only requires a "reasonable degree of care." According to the Perturbation Explanation, removing the clause lowers the standard of care, creating a stricter protection for the disclosing party. The Law Explanation states that this change might be construed as failing to meet the 'reasonable measures' standard required by CUTSA, especially if the receiving party's security practices are lax. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California Civil Code 3426 as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, implying the law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The omission of a higher standard tied to the receiving party's own practices potentially weakens this protection, thus creating a contradiction with CUTSA as explained.

perturbation.original_text: All information described in a CITR and marked with a "confidential," "proprietary," or similar legend shall be deemed Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Only information described in a CITR and explicitly labeled as 'Confidential Information' by the disclosing party shall be deemed Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change narrows the definition of confidential information. Originally, anything marked with 'confidential,' 'proprietary,' or similar was protected. The modification requires an explicit label of 'Confidential Information,' potentially excluding information that the disclosing party intended to be confidential but labeled differently (e.g., 'trade secret').
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1644 states that the words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. By mandating a *specific* label ('Confidential Information'), the modification potentially disregards the parties' intent if they used similar terms to denote confidentiality, conflicting with this principle of contract interpretation.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract text introduces a contradiction by narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Originally, anything marked 'confidential,' 'proprietary,' or similar was protected. The changed text mandates a specific label ('Confidential Information'), potentially excluding information that the disclosing party intended to be confidential but labeled differently. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 1644, states that contract words should be understood in their ordinary and popular sense unless the parties use them in a technical sense or a special meaning is given by usage. By requiring a specific label, the modification potentially disregards the parties' intent if they used similar terms to denote confidentiality, conflicting with the principle of contract interpretation outlined in CIV 1644.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written approval of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "without the prior written approval of the disclosing party" eliminates the possibility of obtaining consent for disclosure. While it strengthens the *prohibition* on disclosure, it also removes a mechanism that could be beneficial in certain circumstances and agreed upon by both parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly counterintuitive, California Business and Professions Code § 16600 broadly prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind. By *completely* prohibiting the disclosure of confidential information to *any* third party, without exception or allowance for prior written consent, this could potentially be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint on trade in certain situations. For example, if the receiving party needs to consult with a specialist or contractor for a legitimate business purpose related to the confidential information, but even with consent, such disclosure is forbidden by the contract, it could be argued that this clause is overly broad and therefore an unenforceable restraint on trade under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the possibility for the disclosing party to grant written approval for the receiving party to disclose confidential information. The LLM-generated Law Explanation posits that this absolute prohibition, without exception, might be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade under California Business and Professions Code § 16600, particularly if the receiving party needs to consult with specialists or contractors. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While the original clause allowed for disclosure with prior written approval, the changed text removes this possibility, creating a situation where a necessary business consultation could be blocked, potentially violating BPC 16600(a).
